ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Nimbu Pani exists; closest defensible match is lemonade (FOODON_03311737). Nimbu Pani is the Indian variant with added black salt and cumin.
ontology.foodex2_description: Lemonade and similar citrus-based soft drinks
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP used as closest defensible category for citrus-based sweetened beverages. No exact Nimbu Pani entry exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Nimbu Pani or Indian lemonade as a food product.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Nimbu Pani is approximately 250 ml (one glass), which is the typical amount served at home or at street stalls across India.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (NIN), ICMR, India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nimbu Pani is not listed as a standalone entry in NIN tables. Values are adapted from NIN lemon juice and sugar water component data, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 167747, lemon juice raw) for micronutrient verification. The standard recipe assumes approximately 30 ml lemon juice, 15 g sugar, 1 g salt, and water to 250 ml, yielding approximately 22 kcal per 100 g of beverage.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Nimbu Pani. GI of 35 is estimated by analogy to lemon juice-based sweetened beverages and dilute sugar solutions documented in the University of Sydney GI Database. Lemon juice itself has a GI near 20; the addition of sugar raises the composite GI to approximately 35 at standard recipe dilution. Glycemic load per 250 ml serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (35 × 13.75) / 100 ≈ 4.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from added salt and black salt (kala namak)
hypertension.guidance: Nimbu Pani prepared with standard added salt and black salt contributes approximately 213 mg sodium per 250 ml serving; individuals with hypertension should request a low-salt or salt-free preparation and monitor cumulative daily sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added sugar content and low glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: When prepared without added sugar or with a non-nutritive sweetener, Nimbu Pani has a negligible glycemic load and is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the standard sweetened version (GL ≈ 4.8 per serving) should be consumed in moderation and counted within the daily carbohydrate allowance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Vitamin C content and negligible saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Nimbu Pani is virtually fat-free and provides approximately 30 mg of Vitamin C per 250 ml serving, which supports endothelial function; however, individuals with cardiovascular disease should opt for the low-salt preparation to avoid excess sodium.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Vitamin C and potential anti-inflammatory properties of lemon juice
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The high Vitamin C content may support antioxidant status; individuals sensitive to citrus acidity should avoid or limit consumption.

gerd.key_factor: Citric acid content of lemon juice
gerd.guidance: The citric acid in lemon juice can trigger or worsen acid reflux symptoms; individuals with GERD should avoid Nimbu Pani or consult their physician before consuming.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nimbu Pani is the quintessential Indian lemonade, consumed across all regions of India as a natural thirst-quencher, particularly during the hot summer months. It is prepared by mixing freshly squeezed lemon juice with water and a sweetener such as sugar or honey, then seasoned with a pinch of regular salt and, characteristically, black salt (kala namak) and roasted cumin powder (bhuna jeera). These spices impart a distinctive tangy, smoky, and slightly sulphurous depth that sets it apart from Western lemonade. It is served in three classic styles: Meetha (sweet), Namkeen (salty), and the most popular Meetha-Namkeen (sweet-salty). When carbonated water is used, it becomes Nimbu Soda or Banta. Nutritionally, Nimbu Pani is a very low-calorie beverage at approximately 22 kcal per 100 g, with minimal protein (0.1 g) and fat (0.05 g), and moderate carbohydrates from added sugar (5.5 g per 100 g). It is a meaningful source of Vitamin C (approximately 12 mg per 100 g, or 30 mg per standard 250 ml serving), supporting immune function and antioxidant status. The glycemic index is estimated at 35 (low), making it suitable for most individuals when sugar is controlled. Sodium content varies significantly with preparation; the standard salted version contributes approximately 213 mg sodium per serving, which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension. It is naturally vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free. Clinically, it is best consumed without added sugar by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, and with minimal salt by those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. It is contraindicated or should be limited in individuals with severe GERD due to its citric acid content.
common_user_queries: Is Nimbu Pani good for diabetes?
How many calories in Nimbu Pani?
What is the difference between Nimbu Pani and lemonade?
Can I drink Nimbu Pani if I have high blood pressure?
How much Vitamin C is in Nimbu Pani?
What is kala namak in Nimbu Pani?

confidence_reason: Nimbu Pani is not listed as a standalone entry in NIN or USDA databases. Nutrient values are adapted from NIN lemon juice and sugar water component data, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central lemon juice raw entry (FDC ID 167747). The GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for similar citrus-sweetened beverages. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from NIN (ICMR) component data for lemon juice and sugar water, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 167747, lemon juice raw). No standalone Nimbu Pani entry exists in any approved primary source. 2. Sodium content (85 mg per 100 g) reflects a standard Meetha-Namkeen preparation with approximately 1 g salt per 250 ml serving; salt-free preparations will have sodium near 5 mg per 100 g. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — plant-based beverage with no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed per USDA FoodData Central lemon juice entry). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no dietary vitamin D source present in this beverage). trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no hydrogenated fats used). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — entirely plant-based beverage). 4. protein_g (0.1 g) and fat_g (0.05 g) are near-trace values consistent with dilute lemon juice; they are not zero and are supported by USDA FoodData Central lemon juice data. 5. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Nimbu Pani exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. Portions in the input JSON contained four identical duplicate entries; deduplicated to one standard serving of 250 ml. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.01), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.01), and saturated_fat_g (0.01) are trace estimates consistent with lemon juice fatty acid profile per USDA FoodData Central; these are near-zero but not confirmed absolute zero. 8. water_g estimated at 93.5 g per 100 g based on mass balance (100 - 5.5 carbohydrate - 0.1 protein - 0.05 fat - 0.1 fibre - 0.085 sodium equivalent ≈ 93.5 g).
